none: A large building with a British flag over the door. The shutters behind a few of the many large windows begin to open.

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: After being introduced to Reynolds in all his fastidious, fashionable glory, the camera turns outward. Whereas he is shot in a tilt, the camera pans downward to reveal the House of Woodcock. The concept of the “house” will be central to Phantom Thread for several reasons. The majority of the film takes place in either Reynolds’s London home or his country home. They both represent aspects of the fashion designer. For instance, the London house is just as orderly and pristine as its owner, reflecting his values and preferences. In this sense, the house is a manifestation of Reynolds himself, not unlike the warehouse is for Barry in Punch-Drunk Love. PTA furthermore plays on the tradition of “haunted houses,” as the ghosts of the past haunt Reynolds, particularly that of his mother, whose affection he deeply misses and whom he seeks to replace with a romantic partner.

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: The flag of the United Kingdom in this scene, like other signifiers later in the film (for example, Reynolds’s particularly British breakfast), establishes early on his embodiment of English ideals.

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: In an interview, PTA shared what drew him to England: “There’s always that old Anglo-fascination...I’d always loved the idea of post-war London, probably from all those movies I love: David Lean’s Brief Encounter [1945] and The Passionate Friends [1949], Michael Powell’s I Know Where I’m Going! [1945]. These are food and drink to me. The Passionate Friends always seems to suffer in comparison to Brief Encounter, but I love it” (Bell, “Paul Thomas Anderson on Phantom Thread”). As James Bell, points out, The Passionate Friends shares many details with Phantom Thread, including a complicated romance, a Swiss holiday, and a New Year’s Eve party at the Chelsea Arts Club Ball. In a post-screening Q&A, Daniel Day-Lewis noted that I Know Where I’m Going! was the source for the idea of the curse in Phantom Thead (Xixax TV, “PHANTOM THREAD - London Q&A”). Elsewhere, PTA also references Dragonwyck (1946) as a precursor to his film (Woodward, “Paul Thomas Anderson”).

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: The house had been essentially gutted by its previous owner, leaving the production team with the challenge and benefit of crafting the interiors, wiring, and so on to fit their needs. The production was furthermore limited by being unable to install a lift and by only being able to work from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. with no exceptions (Lux Lighting, “Lighting Phantom Thread”).
